speaker: interviewer
text: What they were making at the factory that you worked at in Reinsdorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: The factory, actually they were trying to make, they tried to make synthetic benzines. They didn't have enough gas. So they tried to make it from coals. And the factory was meant to make it from coals. It was a big factory. There were 30,000 or however many people. They had their own money, their own-- it was a big factory. But it was destroyed. It was bombed. It would usually-- when we were there, it was bombed daytime by Americans, and night by the British.

speaker: interviewee
text: So only they began to work. They tried to rebuild it. And only the first load came out. At nighttime, the British came and they bombed it. They bombed it. So it didn't work, actually. It didn't give some production. But during that time, there was bombing going on all the time. And during the bombings, they didn't allow us, the Jewish prisoners, to leave the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: They would allow all the others. They had forced-- forced laborers from all over the world was there, working there. But they were allowed to leave when it was a bombing going on, but not we. We would stay in the factory and wait for our fate, what will happen. What will happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived in the tent camp at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, we lived in the tent camp until we went to the other camp. And the condition-- condition was the same, only it wasn't a tent, it was-- it was a barracks. But the conditions were horrible. We didn't have food. People would die every day. They would get-- people would feel how long they'll survive. Some people would say, you know, young people, they would come as skeletons. And they would say, I'll survive a-- a week, or two, or a month. And then came again the selections. And, again, they picked up the people who were weak or sick, and take them to Buchenwald to the gas chambers. So we were less and less.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you happen to be evacuated to Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: So when the Americans came closer, already it was in '45, '45 in spring, late spring '45. Americans came close to Buchenwald. So they took us on a train. It was a small train. And we didn't know where they'll take us. By the train was bombed. And from that point, we went by foot. It was a so-called death march. We went 2,000 people they took us. And we arrived into Litomerice about 800. It was hunger, every day 30, 40 kilometers by foot. We didn't have absolutely what to eat. We would get-- we would stop somewhere. We would get a few boxes of potatoes for the whole group. So it would be for everyone maybe half of a potato for the whole day. We would eat leaves from the trees if we could-- we could reach it. When it was bombings going on, we would catch a potato. But the old-- the elders there, there was-- the officer was Schmidt, he would kill on the spot if he would see-- there was many cases when he killed people because they took a potato, a raw potato. And so we were going. And on the way, people didn't have the strength to continue to go. Some would say, I'll rest for a little. I'll rest for a while. They would sit down. And behind would come [NON-ENGLISH], would come the Hitler-Jugend and the [NON-ENGLISH] they called, the old Germans, and very young from the Hitler-Jugend. And they would kill the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So if you would stop, that was all. I didn't have more the strength to hold my hands. I hold my hands inside my-- inside my-- my-- my prisoner clothes. Because it was so heavy for me I couldn't pass them. Couldn't carry them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we would go-- who would go barefoot, and others would go, because they gave us wooden shoes. And it was with blisters on the feet. We couldn't go, and then never to eat. So-- and when we came there in Litomerice, only 800, a lot of us was sick, and was taken to Theresienstadt the next day already by wagons. And they died after the march.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came, when we arrived in Litomerice, and we were taken to Theresienstadt-- we didn't know where they were taking us. And we arrived there, we saw children, women, Jewish. We were thinking then, there is-- there are no more Jewish left. No one is alive more. And we were thinking then our heart is as a stone. We don't have more feelings. But when we saw the-- the women and children, we cried. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when we came there, they gave us something to eat because there was a Red Cross supplying the camp, and something leftover for the prisoners. They would take it away, the Germans. But something was leftover. And they gave us something to eat. And then began in the typhoid fever-- typhoid fever, and I got sick there. I would lie again on the bunk bed. I didn't feel nothing. I couldn't eat. I was only drinking hot water, giving-- gave all my-- my portion what I received, my bread and so on. And I would give it a man only one for whom was-- was healthy. And he would boil water for us. He had enough to eat. We would give him all our-- our food. And then liberation came. It was the end of the war. It was the 8th of May, 1945. When the liberation came, I didn't feel nothing. I-- I couldn't-- I didn't realize-- I heard only that some people are singing, dancing outside. And they said, oh, the liberation come, you know, already. I couldn't move. I didn't feel.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was so lying for a few days. And then part of my brain was still working. And I realized that if I'll stay there, I'll-- I'll be dead. And it wasn't my intention, you know, to get dead after all what I-- what I-- what I got over. So I mobilized all my strength. And I fell from the bed. And crawling, I went to the outside on the-- on the steps. And I sat down on a window sill. And so I was sitting. And then a man came. He was my roommate in the block, in the same block where I was in the camp. And he was from Hungary, a doctor. So he said, what are you doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, you see what I'm doing here, awaiting my death. He says, I'll help you. So he took me to the hospital. They were group forming, taking to the hospital. He took me to the hospital. And there were a lots of people died there, surviving all the horrors and dying there. I saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after-- the hospital was run by the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was liberated by the Russians. But there were-- all the blocks were by the Red Cross. But the Russians came in. And they asked, who is born in Russia? Who is-- who is from Russia? And we were already occupied by the Russians. So we had to say we are from Riga. And they took us in a separate special block, and it was from special for the Russians. There were some prisoners from-- Russian prisoners, and they took us there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were lying there in that thing only after when we-- when we were-- already we were-- you know, get over this, the typhoid fever came such an appetite. You know, you want to eat. They didn't give a lot to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we couldn't complain, because they said, we are giving you the same-- the same ration, you know, as the soldiers. It wasn't enough for us. So we went to the other blocks. And we got bread. And we would eat day and night from bread, you know. We sold whatever only to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when did you leave Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they send us-- they took-- the Russians took some in the army. But they didn't take us because we were-- we were connected with Russia after the 29. So they asked, who is after 29? It was Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So their part-- they gave us a letter and send us to someplace in Germany already. We went over the border to [NON-ENGLISH]. And we went six-- six people, six young Jewish men from the camp. And we went there. And then we decide on the way, what are we going as cattle, not knowing where we are going, to whom we are going? And we decided to turn around and go to Prague, to Czechoslovakia. So we went. And then an officer helped us. He-- we stood there and said, what are you looking for? We said, we have to go to look for our parents. And look for-- we told him this story, we are from the camps, and liberated from the camps. And we are looking for relatives. We want to go to Prague. So he put us in-- in the-- in a car. He stopped the car. He was an officer there. He stopped the car, a Russian officer. He stopped the car and he put in the car. We went already in the car. He said, be well, [NON-ENGLISH]. It means brothers, Jewish or Israel-- sons of Israel. And he was a Jew also. We didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we went to Prague. And we were there for a month out there in Prague, and eating day and night. And then for the six of us, I was thinking that my father is still in Riga, because I met people who told me then where he was. They were liberated already a few months ago in March of 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my only way was back to Riga to be reunited with my father, the only one who could be survived. So-- and another-- another friend of mine, Jola Weinberg, both of us went to Riga. And the other four, two of them to Israel, two in other places. And then they came to the United States. So I went-- I went to Riga, but on the way, as we crossed the border of Russia in [NON-ENGLISH], they took us to a detention camp. A detention camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were questioned, taking fingerprints, and asking such questions as not how we survived, but why we survived. And then I went to Riga. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't found my father. Father was killed the last day before liberation. He couldn't handle the-- the death march they had. And he stopped at one place. And the next time the group who left was liberated, he was killed. So I lost the last-- I lost my father, whole family. And I was alone in Riga on a family about 40 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was alone, 17 and a half years old. And there come the question, what to do? So I went-- I went to Kaiserwald. And I was standing there. I didn't have to whom to talk. And I said to myself, what will be your future, what you'll do now? And I decided to continue to-- to live, because I felt a sacred duty is to continue family, to show them that they didn't manage to suppress us, to make for us un-human.

speaker: interviewee
text: I still had my dignity, my self respect. And I decided to continue to live, whatever it will cost. And I went to the city. It was very hard-- it was very conditions after the war there. I went to study. I went to work. I met my friend, my former girlfriend. And she was-- she's a neighbor, she was a neighbor and we went to the same school. She survived all the horrors also. And she had her own story to tell. And united our lives, created a new family [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with-- and we began a new life. There were children born, two daughters. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and so-- so went on, life went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed in Riga until-- you couldn't get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stayed in Riga. The Riga was a trap. You came in, but out you couldn't get out. So I-- had connections with my aunt from United States, from New York. I had correspondence with her. And it was over certain time. And she tried. She said she'll do everything me to get out. But it was impossible. And then begin again. Again began the-- the persecution of the Jewish people again from the communist side, from Stalin. And we had to hide again. And we felt again that we are Jewish, wherever we are, was it Germany, was it Riga, was it Russia, you have the same-- the same conditions. And we tried to-- to hide, because there was a time when they made for false accusations about the doctors who were to kill the-- Stalin and all the other Russian people. And the first place they were sent to Siberia, it was the people who survived from the concentration camps. So we left our house. And we went to France. And we're hiding. And the only what helped us was the death of Stalin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Things got better then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, better, but at least we survived again this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in 1980 you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, after hard try-- we tried for 10 years. The children-- the children grew up. We tried to give them traditional Jewish education at home. It wasn't possible to have the education at schools, because schools, there was not-- no Jewish schools, not whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we tried it at home, whatever we could. And things got-- they got married to Jewish man. They have children. And so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think that we did everything what we could to continue our family, our people, our traditions, our life. Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to introduce my family, not my whole family. The other members of the family are not able to come today. My older daughter, she lives in San Diego and works. And she's not able to come. It's a pity. But we can nothing do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here on the right, it's my wife and friend Doba. And on the left, it's my younger daughter, my [NON-ENGLISH]. It's Elizabeth. And it's my son-in-law, her husband, Ilya. And here is my older grandson, Label, or [NON-ENGLISH], or Leon. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the people who perished, most of them were Yiddish speaking. And they died with Yiddish words on their lips. I want to read a poem written by Moishe [NON-ENGLISH], and it's about a mountain of shoes he saw in the camp of Majdanek. He says, he saw a mountain of shoes, thousands of them. Shoes from grandchildren and grandfathers, shoes from brides and grooms who didn't make to the [NON-ENGLISH]. Shoes from rabbis and ordinary people, shoes small, [NON-ENGLISH] booties of babies who only began to walk. And, suddenly, a miracle happened. All the thousands of shoes lined up by size, by pairs, in rows. And they moved. He saw the whole mountain moving. And they were only-- the only live witnesses what left over from the millions of people perished in the concentration camps in the gas chambers. He said-- he says, and the shoes, they avoided the hellfire only because they are from stuff and leather, not from flesh and blood. So now as last witnesses, they want to go to be further generations for the years to come. And he says, they weren't able-- the murderers weren't able to take him in his [NON-ENGLISH]. So now he says, let everyone hear the steps which flow as tears. The steps that measure out the judgment. And he says, you can hear in the noise of the steps, the cries and sobs of the children and women perished in the gas chambers. [YDDISH] This picture is taken in Riga in 1920. On the-- on the left-- from the left to the right, my mother, Guta standing. The other one my mother's sister, my aunt Eda. Sitting, from the left to the right, my grandfather, Yakob, after whom I am named, and my uncle Chaim Gleser. The whole family is Glesers. This picture is taken in Riga in 1930. On the left, from left to right, my father Leizer. That's me, three years old, [NON-ENGLISH], and mother Guta, all Basners. This picture is taken [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1934 end, or beginning of 1935. The left is sitting there is the twin brother and sister of mine born in 1934. And this is Celia and Abe. Both of them perished when they were seven years old in the forest of [NON-ENGLISH].
